  • Evaluation of the models

Simulation experiment (1)


Design of the experiment

We simulate data according to the following method (\(X\) = non-reference method, \(Y\) = reference method). We first simulate a latent (true) value for the non-reference method using a normal distribution: \(X_{\mathrm{true}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x^2)\) and then use this value to simulate a value for the latent reference method, assuming a linear relation between both methods and some structural noise that is not due to measurement error: \(Y_{\mathrm{true}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\alpha + \beta X_{\mathrm{true}}, \sigma_y^2)\). We then add measurement noise to both methods (resulting from both field and lab measurement processes). In the notation below we assume bivariate normal measurement error, but it is defined analogously for bivariate Student-\(t\) distributed measurement error: \[ \begin{pmatrix} X_{\mathrm{obs}}\\ Y_{\mathrm{obs}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \begin{pmatrix} X_{\mathrm{true}}\\ Y_{\mathrm{true}} \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_u^2\ & \rho\sigma_u\sigma_v \\ \rho\sigma_u\sigma_v & \sigma_v^2 \end{pmatrix} \right) \] The measurement noise magnitudes are expressed as fractions of the SD of the latent \(X\): \[ \begin{aligned} \sigma_u &= \tau_x\sigma_x \\ \sigma_v &= \tau_{xy}\sigma_u \end{aligned} \] In this experiment we focus on the effect of the sample size of the training dataset (\(N\)), the tails of the distribution of the measurement error and the ratio between the SD of the measurement error and the SD of the latent variable (\(\tau_x\)). Other parameter values are kept fixed:

Table: Parameter values for the scenario’s of experiment 1.
Parameter Values
\(N\) 100, 200, 500
\(\mu_x\) 0
\(\sigma_x\) 1
\(\alpha\) 0
\(\beta\) 1
\(\sigma_y\) 0.1
Tails measurement error Normal & Student-\(t\) (dof=3) distribution
\(\tau_x\) 0.01, 0.1, 0.2
\(\tau_{xy}\) 1
\(\rho\) 0


Description of the models

We fit the following 3 Bayesian models on each dataset. The models are fitted with the cmdstanr package.

Linear regression

We assume a Gaussian likelihood for the observed data and a linear relation with the predictor. The priors are chosen to reflect prior knowledge on TF where we can reasonably expect the intercept to lie around zero and the slope to be around 1 (negative values of the slope are excluded). Without loss of generality, we assume that the predictor variable (the non-reference method) is centered and scaled to zero-mean and unit-variance. The prior on the SD of the residuals is chosen to be log-normal with zero-mean and SD 0.5; this is a rather informative prior and was chosen to be aligned with the EIV-models where an informative prior was necessary to deal with the non-identifiability problem.

Priors: \[ \begin{aligned} \beta_0 &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1^2) \\ \beta_1 &\sim \mathcal{N}^+(1, 0.5^2) \\ \sigma_y &\sim \log\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5) \\ \end{aligned} \] Likelihood: \[ y_{\text{obs},i} \sim \mathcal{N}(\beta_0 + \beta_1x_{\text{obs},i}, \sigma_y^2) \]

EIV-regression with known SD of measurement error

In this model, we assume we know the true value of the SD of the measurement error in \(X\) (\(\sigma_u\)) and we pass it to the model. The EIV framework uses a hierarchical model where at the first layer, we assume the latent predictor comes from a normal distribution with mean \(\mu_x\) and SD \(\sigma_x\) and we assign priors to these parameters. The other priors stay as before.

Priors: \[ \begin{aligned} \mu_x &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \\ \sigma_x &\sim \log\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5) \\ \beta_0 &\sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1^2) \\ \beta_1 &\sim \mathcal{N}^+(1, 0.5^2) \\ \sigma_y &\sim \log\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5) \end{aligned} \]

Because we use Gaussian distributions for the latent predictor, they can be integrated out, giving the following: \[ \begin{aligned} \tilde{v} &= (\sigma_x^{-2} + \sigma_u^{-2}))^{-1} \\ \tilde{\mu}_i &= \tilde{v} (\sigma_x^{-2} \mu_x + \sigma_u^{-2}x_i) \\ \tilde{\sigma}_y^2 &= \sigma_y^2 + \beta_1^2 \tilde{v} \\ \end{aligned} \] The likelihood is now composed of two terms. The weighted (shrunk) predictor \(\tilde{\mu}_i\) is now used in the linear regression model and the effect of using a latent variable as predictor results in the residual variance being increased proportional with \(\beta_1^2\): \[ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{obs},i} &\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x) \\ y_{\text{obs},i} &\sim \mathcal{N}(\beta_0 + \beta_1\tilde{\mu}_i, \tilde{\sigma}_y^2) \end{aligned} \]

EIV-regression with prior on SD of measurement error

Same as above, the only difference is that we need to assign a (strong) prior to the unknown SD of the measurement error for which we choose the same lognormal distribution as before: \(\sigma_u \sim \log\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5)\).

Priors:

The following figure shows the priors used in the different models:

Model diagnostics: MCMC \(\hat{R}\)

The following figures display the \(\hat{R}\) statistic of the MCMC for each of the model parameters. The red dashed lines are at 1.00 and 1.01. The boxplots are aggregated across scenario’s because the values were similar in each of them and all well below 1.01.
